title: The Art of Being Present with Whatever Arises in Psychedelic Space
body:
The Art of Being Present with Whatever Arises in Psychedelic Space

There I was, lying on a simple foam mattress in a softly lit room, the subtle hum of a Tibetan singing bowl lingering in the air. My eyes were closed, but images flooded my mind like a vivid film reel played by an unseen director. At one point, a deep wave of fear crept in - a raw, almost overwhelming anxiety that shook me to the core. My first instinct was to resist, to push it away as something unwanted, dangerous. But then something shifted. Instead of fighting, I leaned into the fear, letting it wash over me like a tide, no longer an enemy but a strange companion. What happened next surprised me: The fear lost its sharp edges and transformed into a deep insight, a kind of somatic wisdom that felt both ancient and immediate. That moment - the art of being completely present with whatever arises during a psychedelic journey - became a defining lesson in my practice and understanding of these medicines.

Most trips, especially with mushrooms or psilocybin, come with an unpredictable emotional terrain. You might encounter bliss, sorrow, confusion, or that sudden pulse of terror. What sets apart life-changing experiences from terrifying ones is often the ability to stay anchored, to observe without judgment. This isn’t about control; it’s about surrendering with awareness. In the 15 years I’ve worked with these substances, both personally and professionally, I’ve learned that the true medicine often lies in those moments of discomfort. The capacity to be present with whatever arises - that’s where healing, insight, and change take root.

We talk a lot about set and setting in psychedelic therapy circles, but I think the inner setting - the attitude we bring toward our experience - is just as crucial. It’s not just about preparing the body or the room, but training the mind to greet every sensation, every emotion, every vision with a kind kind of curiosity and acceptance. This art of presence can turn a chaotic trip into a sacred dialogue with the self, a chance to meet your mind’s darkest corners and your heart’s brightest light all at once. And it is this skill, the willingness to stay awake and open no matter what, that can make psychedelic therapy a powerful tool for lasting change.

Understanding the Core Concept: Presence Amidst Psychedelic Experience

So, what does it really mean to be present in psychedelic space? At its simplest, it means showing up fully in the now with whatever the experience brings. No running, no hiding. The essence of this practice finds roots in ancient mindfulness traditions, which teach nonjudgmental awareness of the present moment. Psychedelics often strip away our usual mental filters and defenses, exposing raw emotion and thought patterns that we normally keep buried. Presence, then, becomes the ability to witness these without being swept away or overwhelmed.

From a neuroscience standpoint, researchers like Robin Carhart-Harris have shown how psychedelics reduce activity in the brain’s default mode network (DMN), the hub of self-referential thinking and rumination. This quieting of the DMN correlates with a loosening of rigid thought patterns and increased openness to experience. But with that openness comes vulnerability. The usual mental walls go down, and without the practice of presence, the flood of sensations and thoughts can feel like drowning.

Presence is like a steady observer inside your mind, a calm awareness that notices the waves without being pulled under. It’s not suppressing difficult feelings but holding them gently, allowing them space to exist. This is why many therapists emphasize the importance of "sitting with" whatever arises during a session rather than trying to fix or avoid it. It creates a kind of psychological safety where deep processing can happen.

In the words of Stanislav Grof, the father of modern psychedelic therapy, the capacity to face “the death-rebirth experience” with openness and presence is what transforms psychological trauma into healing. The physical sensations, emotional upheavals, and visionary states that occur during psychedelic sessions aren’t just random - they are part of a meaningful symbolic journey. Being present lets us move through that journey instead of getting lost in it.

Scientific Studies on Presence and Psychedelic Therapy

There’s a growing body of research that supports the role of presence in enhancing the therapeutic impact of psychedelics. One of the most cited studies in this arena comes from Roland Griffiths and his team at Johns Hopkins University. In their landmark 2016 paper, participants who received psilocybin reported “mystical-type experiences” that correlated with lasting positive changes in behavior and mood. What was striking was that those who embraced the experience fully - including the difficult parts - tended to report the most deep benefits.

Another study by Carhart-Harris and colleagues at Imperial College London looked at brain function in people undergoing psilocybin therapy for depression. They observed that reduced DMN activity allowed patients to gain a fresh perspective on their thoughts and feelings. This decoupling from habitual mental loops, paired with a mindful attitude, was essential for the therapeutic breakthroughs they experienced.

In 2020, a fascinating trial by Davis et al. explored how participants’ emotional acceptance during psilocybin sessions related to outcomes in anxiety and depression. They found that those who practiced acceptance - essentially being present with difficult emotions - showed higher reductions in symptoms afterward. This directly supports the idea that presence during challenging moments can enhance healing.

There’s also a wealth of anecdotal evidence from practitioners like James Fadiman, who popularized microdosing, and Paul Stamets, who’s emphasized the importance of integration and mindful awareness in mushroom experiences. Their work highlights how presence isn’t just a nice concept - it’s a practical tool that shapes the overall quality and benefits of the psychedelic encounter.

How to Practice Presence During Your Psychedelic Sessions

Applying presence in psychedelic space is a skill that can be developed intentionally, just like any form of meditation or inner work. It doesn’t mean you have to be flawless or reach some perfect state of calm. Instead, it’s about coming back to the moment, again and again, as sensations and thoughts arise. Here are some practical steps to cultivate this art of being present:

  1. Set an intention around openness. Before your session, remind yourself that you will meet whatever comes with curiosity, not resistance. This sets the mental tone.
  2. Focus on your breath. When emotions or visions spike, gently returning your attention to the breath anchors you in your body. This simple act keeps you grounded.
  3. Use gentle self-talk. If fear or discomfort surfaces, talk to yourself like a compassionate friend. “I see you. I’m here with you.” This softens inner resistance.
  4. Allow emotions to flow. Instead of labeling feelings as good or bad, watch them arise and pass like clouds. Notice their physical sensations too - tightness, warmth, tingling.
  5. Keep a journal nearby. Writing about your experience as it unfolds, or right after, can deepen presence and understanding. I rely on a simple Journaling Notebook Leather Bound to capture the rawness and insight that emerge.

Remember, presence is not about forcing yourself to stay calm - there will be moments of overwhelm. Instead, it’s about gently returning your attention, noticing what’s real in this moment. As Michael Pollan writes in How to Change Your Mind, “the experience can be challenging, but if you let it be, it can be redemptive.” This letting be is the heart of presence.

Common Obstacles in Staying Present and How to Overcome Them

It’s one thing to talk about staying present; it’s another to actually do it when your mind and body are riding the psychedelic waves. I’ve seen many people struggle with this, myself included. The challenges can be intense anxiety, physical discomfort, confusion, or feeling lost in the experience. Here are some of the toughest obstacles and ways I’ve learned to work through them:

Resisting uncomfortable emotions: Fear or sadness can feel like quicksand. The urge to escape is natural. But pushing away these feelings often makes them louder. Instead, I recommend expanding your inner space so that these emotions have room to move. Try softening your gaze inward and naming the feeling (“This is fear”), then breathe into it. This mindful naming often reduces the emotion’s grip.

Overthinking and mental looping: Many trip moments come with repeated thoughts or worries. A persistent story plays on repeat in your mind. When this happens, try shifting attention to your senses - notice the texture of a blanket, the sound of a voice, the rise and fall of your chest. This sensory grounding cuts through thought spirals.

Physical discomfort or restlessness: Sometimes the body aches or jitters, and stillness seems impossible. Gentle movement, like stretching or changing position, can help. If that’s not an option, focusing on your breath or scanning your body with awareness can ease tension.

Feeling disconnected or dissociated: Occasionally, psychedelics can induce a sense of detachment from self or environment. Here, gently reminding yourself of your physical presence - touching something tangible or noticing your heartbeat - can help re-anchor you.

These strategies are not quick fixes but invitations to practice patience with yourself. The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk offers important insights on how trauma - often processed during psychedelic therapy - can trigger these responses. Healing takes time, and presence is the cornerstone of that patience.

Integrating Presence into Daily Life Beyond the Trip

What’s remarkable is how the art of presence developed during psychedelic experiences can ripple out into everyday living. Many people report that their ability to stay awake and open in sessions translates into greater mindfulness off the medicine. Instead of reacting on autopilot, they learn to pause and witness life’s challenges with more ease.

Integration practices often emphasize this. Journaling about your sessions, for example, solidifies lessons and deepens self-awareness. I keep a trusted leather-bound notebook close at hand not only for sessions but for daily reflections. Writing down moments where you managed to stay present helps build resilience and confidence.

Mindfulness meditation is another powerful way to maintain presence. Even just a few minutes each day of focused breathing or body scanning can train your mind to be less reactive and more grounded. Researchers like Judson Brewer have demonstrated how mindfulness can reduce anxiety and improve emotional regulation - skills that psychedelic therapy often brings into sharper focus.

Over the long term, cultivating presence fosters deeper intimacy with yourself and others. You become more attuned to subtle feelings and less prone to judgment. This echoes the work of Grof and others who describe psychedelics as tools for accessing the "deep self." Staying present is what lets you sit with that deep self, learning to listen rather than flee.

A Final Thought: Presence as the Bridge Between Worlds

Here’s a thought to sit with: Presence in psychedelic space is not just a technique or a coping skill. It’s a kind of bridge - a bridge between the ordinary self and the vast unknown inside us. When you are fully present, you become both the seeker and the witness, the storm and the calm. You move beyond the usual boundaries of your mind and enter a space where insight, healing, and connection unfold spontaneously.

But this bridge is fragile. It demands courage and gentleness at once. It asks us to bear witness to our own depths without flinching. In that vulnerability lies a strange kind of strength, a quiet affirmation that whatever arises is part of the process. It’s not something to be fixed or escaped, but something to be received.

As someone who has grown through countless journeys with mushrooms and other psychedelics, I’ve come to see presence as the true medicine. It’s not in the visions or revelations alone, but in the simple act of being with yourself fully. That art - the willingness to stay present with whatever arises - is a gift worth practicing, beyond any trip, beyond any moment. It is a way of living and healing that can carry us through both the wild spaces of our inner worlds and the everyday moments of our lives.

 So, you’re sitting quietly, the world feels different, colors seem sharper, sounds more alive. You’re in psychedelic space. Maybe...
